era_context: 1738 was a year of significant military and political activity. The Russo-Turkish War (1735–1739) was ongoing, with Russian forces advancing in the Caucasus and Black Sea regions. Nadir Shah of Persia was consolidating his power and preparing for his invasion of the Mughal Empire, which would culminate in the sack of Delhi in 1739. In Europe, the War of Austrian Succession was approaching, with tensions rising over the succession to the Holy Roman Empire. The Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor was in its early years of expansion. Colonial rivalries between European powers were intensifying, particularly in the Americas.

events.title: Nadir Shah Invades the Mughal Empire
events.description: Nadir Shah, the Persian ruler, launched his invasion of the Mughal Empire in late 1738, crossing into Indian territory with a large army. The invasion was motivated by territorial ambitions, the desire to plunder Mughal wealth, and the opportunity to exploit Mughal internal instability. Nadir Shah's forces advanced rapidly through northern India, defeating Mughal armies and moving toward Delhi. The invasion would culminate in the sack of Delhi in March 1739, but the campaign began in earnest in 1738.

sources.name: Mirza Mehdi Khan, History of Nadir Shah (contemporary Persian chronicle)
Mughal court records and later Indian historiography

events.certainty_note: The invasion is well-documented in Persian and Mughal sources. The date of the invasion's beginning (late 1738) is confirmed in contemporary accounts.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Russo-Turkish Conflict Continues
events.region: Ottoman Empire and Russian territories (Caucasus, Black Sea)
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War (1735–1739) continued in 1738 with Russian forces advancing in the Caucasus and Black Sea regions. Russian armies under the command of Peter Lacy achieved significant military successes, capturing key fortifications and territories. The Ottoman Empire, weakened by internal administrative problems and military defeats, struggled to mount an effective defense. The war would conclude in 1739 with the Treaty of Belgrade, which established Russia as a major regional power.

sources.name: Russian military records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the war and Russian military successes are well-documented in Russian and Ottoman sources. The date is confirmed.

events.title: Excavation of Herculaneum Begins
events.description: Systematic excavation of the Roman city of Herculaneum, buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 CE, began in 1738 under the patronage of King Charles VII of Naples (later Charles III of Spain). The excavations revealed remarkably well-preserved buildings, artifacts, and organic materials, providing unprecedented insights into Roman daily life. The discovery of Herculaneum, along with the earlier discovery of Pompeii, revolutionized understanding of Roman civilization and contributed to the development of modern archaeology.
events.certainty_note: The beginning of systematic excavation in 1738 is well-documented in contemporary records. The date is confirmed.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor Continues Consolidation of Power
events.description: The Qianlong Emperor, who had ascended to the throne in 1735, continued to consolidate his power and implement administrative reforms in 1738. He strengthened the examination system, expanded the imperial bureaucracy, and initiated military campaigns to expand Qing territorial control. The year 1738 saw the continuation of his policies of centralization and cultural patronage, which would define his long reign as one of the most prosperous and expansive periods of the Qing Dynasty.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and official histories

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of power is documented in Qing records, but 1738 specifically is not marked by a single dramatic event. The certainty is based on the broader pattern of his early reign.

events.title: John Wesley's Religious Conversion
events.description: John Wesley, the English clergyman and theologian, experienced a profound religious conversion on May 24, 1738, at a Moravian meeting in Aldersgate Street, London. Wesley described the experience as a moment of spiritual awakening in which he felt his heart 'strangely warmed.' This conversion marked the beginning of Wesley's evangelical ministry and led to the founding of Methodism. Wesley's conversion and subsequent evangelical activities had profound effects on Protestant Christianity and contributed to the Great Awakening.
key_figures: Charles Wesley (his brother, also involved in the Methodist movement)
events.certainty_note: Wesley's conversion experience is well-documented in his own journal and contemporary accounts. The date (May 24, 1738) is confirmed in Wesley's writings.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of John Wesley's Aldersgate conversion has been reassessed by modern scholarship. While Wesley himself described it as a transformative moment, some historians argue that his evangelical ministry and theological development were more gradual processes, and that the Aldersgate experience, while important, was not as dramatically transformative as Wesley's own account suggests. Additionally, the excavation of Herculaneum, while beginning in 1738, was not as systematic or scientifically rigorous as later excavations; early excavators were more interested in treasure-hunting than in archaeological documentation, and many artifacts and structures were damaged or lost in the process.
historiographic_note: 1738 is well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding military and political events. Russian, Ottoman, British, and Italian sources provide rich detail. Indian and Persian sources document Nadir Shah's invasion preparations. English sources document Wesley's conversion. Potential biases include: Russian sources may emphasize military successes; Ottoman sources may downplay defeats; Persian sources may exaggerate Nadir Shah's achievements; English sources may emphasize Wesley's importance to later Methodist history. The excavation of Herculaneum is well-documented in contemporary records, but early excavation reports may be incomplete or inaccurate by modern standards.

graph_edges.note: Nadir Shah's invasion in 1738 led to the sack of Delhi in 1739
The Russo-Turkish War continued in 1738 and concluded with the Treaty of Belgrade in 1739
The excavation of Herculaneum beginning in 1738 continued and expanded in subsequent years
Wesley's conversion in 1738 led to his field preaching and evangelical ministry in 1739
